petitioner_background: The petitioner is a dermatologist seeking classification as an employment-based immigrant as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. The record reflects the petitioner had received a best poster award at a conference in Bangkok, Thailand, held four patents, claimed 21 published articles (with screenshots showing 18 and 31 articles on different databases), and claimed authorship or co-authorship of five books related to dermatology. The petitioner's academic history included rankings in medical school classes and examinations at institutions including a University of Medical Sciences.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of a best poster award received at a conference in Bangkok, Thailand, along with a screenshot reflecting that the conference was sponsored by one organization in conjunction with another. The petitioner also submitted several appreciation documents regarding academic rankings, including an appreciation plaque acknowledging his second rank in a medical program, an appreciation letter acknowledging his third rank in the field of medicine for academic year 1998-1999, and a document regarding his second rank at a University of Medical Sciences.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO withdrew the director's favorable determination. The AAO found the petitioner did not submit any documentary evidence establishing that the best poster award is a nationally or internationally recognized award for excellence in the field. Submitting evidence of receipt of an award is insufficient without demonstrating that the award is nationally or internationally recognized. Additionally, the AAO noted that the regulation uses the plural ('prizes or awards'), requiring more than one nationally or internationally recognized award, while the director based his decision on only one award. The AAO cited Maramjaya v. USCIS and Snapnames.com Inc. v. Chertoff for the principle that USCIS may interpret significance from singular versus plural usage. Regarding the academic appreciation documents, the AAO found that academic study is not a field of endeavor but training for a future field of endeavor, so academic awards, scholarships, student awards, postdoctoral fellowships, and financial aid awards cannot be considered nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards in the petitioner's field. The petitioner also failed to submit evidence beyond the awarding entities demonstrating the academic awards were recognized nationally or internationally.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of four patents/inventions, claimed to have published 21 articles (with screenshots from one database showing 18 articles and another showing 31 articles, with 48 total citations, highest cited article at 11 citations, and 20 articles never cited), and claimed authorship of five books. The curriculum vitae reflected co-authorship of two books and main authorship of three books, but the petitioner submitted evidence of only two books, with three others marked as 'Under Development.' One book was claimed to have been 'selected as [a] major university book in 18th course of selected university books in 2008.' On appeal, counsel submitted four letters regarding one book, including letters stating the book was selected as a 'major university book in 46th course of selected university books in 2012' and is 'really useful for every general practitioner dealing with dermatologic problems,' plus letters from Dr. [redacted] of a University of Medical Sciences stating he has benefited from the book and is teaching from it.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO affirmed the director's unfavorable determination. Regarding the patents, the AAO held that registration of patents alone does not demonstrate impact or influence on the field; citing Matter of New York State Department of Transportation, a patent recognizes originality but does not demonstrate a contribution of major significance, and significance must be determined case-by-case. Counsel did not contest the director's decision on patents on appeal, so the issue was abandoned. Regarding the published articles, the AAO found that while citations reflect some interest from the field, they are not automatic indicators of major significance. The petitioner's citation count (48 total, highest 11, with 20 articles never cited) was not reflective of major significance, and no documentary evidence showed the articles were unusually influential or discussed in-depth. This issue was also abandoned on appeal. Regarding the books, the AAO found the petitioner went on record without supporting evidence for three claimed books (Matter of Soffici), and eligibility must be established at time of filing (Matter of Katigbak, Matter of Izummi). For the one book addressed on appeal, the translation of the cover reflected the petitioner 'cooperated' with rather than authored the book, and there was no evidence of the nature or extent of his cooperation. The four letters did not overcome concerns about significance — the book's influence was limited to two universities for prospective dermatology students, not widely used throughout the field. The AAO cited Kazarian v. USCIS, 580 F.3d 1030, noting vague, solicited letters from local colleagues that do not specifically identify contributions are insufficient, and cited Matter of Caron International and Matter of V-K- for the principle that solicited letters carry less weight than preexisting independent evidence. Finally, the AAO noted that the regulation requires more than one original contribution of major significance (plural), while counsel only claimed eligibility based on a single contribution.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of authorship of scholarly articles in the field (the decision does not re-describe the specific evidence in this section but references the 18-31 articles shown in database screenshots discussed under the original contributions criterion).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director determined the petitioner established eligibility for this criterion, and the AAO agreed. The AAO found that a review of the record reflected the petitioner submitted sufficient documentary evidence establishing he meets the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vi), which requires evidence of authorship of scholarly articles in the field, in professional or major trade publications or other major media.

outcome_reasoning: The appeal was dismissed. The AAO withdrew the director's favorable determination on the awards criterion, leaving the petitioner with qualifying evidence under only one of the required three categories (scholarly articles). The director's determinations on the one-time achievement, membership, artistic display, leading or critical role, and commercial successes criteria were not contested on appeal and were deemed abandoned (citing Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen. and Hristov v. Roark). The AAO declined to conduct a final merits determination, stating that the proper conclusion under Kazarian is that the petitioner failed to satisfy the antecedent regulatory requirement of three types of evidence. The AAO also separately found, although not addressed by the director, that the petitioner failed to submit clear evidence under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(5) that he intends to come to the United States to continue work in his area of expertise as a dermatologist, as no prospective job letters, contracts, or detailed plans were submitted.
legal_citations: Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
Maramjaya v. USCIS, Civ. Act. No. 06-2158 (RCL) (D.C. Cir. March 26, 2008)
Snapnames.com Inc. v. Chertoff, 2006 WL 3491005 (D. Or. Nov. 30, 2006)
Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28 (3rd Cir. 1995)
Matter of New York State Department of Transportation, 22 I&N Dec. 215 (Comm'r 1998)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg'l Comm'r 1972)
Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45 (Reg'l Comm'r 1971)
Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791 (Comm'r 1988)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issues: (1) Counsel failed to contest the director's adverse findings on the one-time achievement, membership, artistic display, leading or critical role, and commercial successes criteria, and failed to contest the patents and scholarly articles issues under the original contributions criterion, resulting in those issues being deemed abandoned on appeal. (2) The petitioner claimed authorship of five books on his curriculum vitae but submitted evidence of only two; three books were marked 'Under Development' with no evidence they were published at the time of filing, and the AAO noted eligibility must be established at time of filing and cannot be approved based on facts arising after filing. (3) The petitioner claimed to have authored a book but the translated cover reflected he only 'cooperated' with the book, raising a discrepancy about authorship. (4) The petitioner failed to submit any documentary evidence demonstrating intent to continue work in the United States as required by 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(5), an issue not addressed by the director but identified by the AAO.
